feedback.text: The EU member states have long upheld consumer protections, more so than most other places in the world. This act is another example of small yet important changes being mandated to improve the experience of and protect consumers. However, not addressed by this act is another phenomenon that often occurs with digital products and purchases. Rather than sell the users a copy of their product, companies and service providers instead only license access to certain services. Sometimes a user may purchase such a license, only to find that the company decides to no longer offer the service, only a few months or years afterwards. Imagine purchasing a power drill, only to be told you can no longer operate it two years later. It is understandable that some products require constant servicing by the manufacturer, but I believe it to be just as understandable that consumers wish to purchase a product they can rely on and own, rather than a flimsy "it might work in a year or two". The original motivator for this feedback is the Stop Destroying Videogames Initiative ( ECI(2024)000007 ); however, I believe this to be an issue even beyond video games. As a consumer, I wish to purchase and own products, not be locked into obscure agreements. Thank you for taking the time to read this feedback. I hope to have provided valuable insight.
